moves.note: 2x leveraged, small space bet, cut it
Slight trim to fund cash buffer

plan: Sell both leveraged ETFs (SOXL, SPAL) immediately — they are the biggest single-month loss drivers and have no place in a capital-preservation posture.
Trim SMH from 13% to 7% to pull semiconductor exposure down and reduce the sector's outsized drag risk.
Trim CRWD from 12% to 7% so no single name holds more than 10% of the book.
Add to QQQI (income) and SPY (broad index) with the freed capital, keeping both sectors well under the 45% cap.
The remaining freed cash (~15-18% of book) sits as a cash buffer — deploy it only if the market gives a confirmed pullback entry, not proactively.
conservative.read: This posture targets roughly 1-1.5% per month in calmer markets, driven mainly by QQQI distributions and broad-index drift. The honest risk is that even SPY and QQQ can drop 5-10% in a bad month, so the cash buffer is doing real work here — a 2-sigma bad month in this configuration would likely be in the -8 to -10% range rather than the current -17.9%, but it is not zero.

moves.note: Highest scanner score, quality AI name
Top-rated setup, add only on trigger
Free cash for buffer and new entries

plan: Exit SPAL entirely — it is the weakest holding by both performance and scanner conviction, and the freed capital is better used elsewhere.
Trim SOXL to 3% to keep total leveraged ETF exposure under 5% of the book, satisfying the balanced-mode leverage ceiling.
Trim SMH to 9% and SPY to 25% to generate the ~10% cash buffer without over-rotating.
Add a small amount to CRWD (14%) given its +7.4% momentum and top scanner score among current holdings.
Place a conditional Watch on PANW at the scanner entry (351.34-351.69) — only execute if price triggers; do not chase.
The net result is a cash buffer of roughly 10%, total leverage under 5%, and semiconductor concentration trimmed from 19% to about 12%.
balanced.read: A balanced book like this realistically targets 2-3% in a favorable month, blending index drift, QQQI income, and selective tech momentum. The key risk is that tech and semiconductors remain correlated — a sector-wide downturn hits CRWD, QQQI (Nasdaq-linked), and QQQ simultaneously, so the 10% cash buffer is not decoration; it is the first line of defense.

moves.note: Lean into chips if momentum returns
Space 2x small add, scanner ASTS watch
Reduce broad index to fund adds

plan: Add to SOXL (10%) and SMH (15%) to lean into a semiconductor recovery thesis — this is a deliberate concentration increase and raises both upside and drawdown meaningfully.
Add slightly to SPAL (4%) and place a Watch on ASTS at scanner entry (85.93-86.01) as a space-sector satellite position alongside SPAL.
Buy ARM at the scanner entry (336.80-337.13) with stop at 336.18 — it carries the highest reward-to-risk ratio (9.46) of all current setups.
Buy PANW at scanner entry (351.34-351.69) with stop at 350.62 — it holds the top ACCUMULATE rating (9/12) and a 6.01 R score.
Trim SPY to 22% and QQQ to 12% to fund the adds; keep a ~5% cash buffer as a strict floor — do not let it go lower.
Every leveraged and new position must have its scanner stop honored with no exceptions; the aggressive mode only works with rigid stop discipline.
aggressive.read: This configuration could target 4%+ in a strong tech/semiconductor month, but the 2-sigma bad-month drawdown expands well beyond the current -17.9% — with SOXL at 10% and added single names, a correlated tech selloff could push a bad month toward -22 to -25% of equity. Leveraged ETFs like SOXL decay daily through volatility drag, meaning they lose value even in flat choppy markets; this mode demands active daily monitoring and stop execution without hesitation.

moves.note: Leveraged ETF has no place in income…
No yield, pure speculation, exit fully
No income, high-beta chip ETF, exit
Keep broad anchor, slight trim for…
Trim growth ETF, redirect to income

plan: Sell SOXL, SPAL, and SMH in full — none of these pay distributions and all carry high drawdown risk that is inconsistent with an income-first posture.
Trim CRWD to 5% to retain a small growth satellite without letting any single non-income name dominate the book.
Add heavily to QQQI (35%) as the primary income engine — it generates monthly distributions by writing calls on Nasdaq holdings.
Trim QQQ from 18% to 10% and SPY from 30% to 25%, using freed capital to build a cash buffer of roughly 15% alongside the QQQI add.
The resulting book is approximately 35% QQQI, 25% SPY, 10% QQQ, 5% CRWD, and 15% cash — simple, income-weighted, and low-beta.
income.read: This posture targets roughly 1% per month primarily through QQQI's covered-call distributions, supplemented by modest index appreciation in SPY and QQQ. The critical honest caveat is that QQQI's NAV is tied to Nasdaq performance — in a sharp tech selloff the distributions do not disappear immediately but the NAV falls, meaning total return can still be negative even while income payments continue; this is not equivalent to holding cash or a money-market fund.
